name: April Greiman
era: 1970s–present, Made in Space studio, Los Angeles
description: April Greiman was the first major designer to embrace digital tools (late 1980s Mac) — her *Design Quarterly 133* (1986) was a single life-size fold-out produced entirely on MacPaint and is still in design canon. Greiman fuses Swiss rigor with layered imagery, early digital aesthetic, and a New Wave sensibility that Wolfgang Weingart had trained her on at Basel. Distinct from both Vignelli (too-Swiss) and Scher (too-print) — Greiman is the first computer-native designer.
rules: Layer transparency is the signature — overlapping elements with mix-blend-mode or opacity 0.6–0.8, echoes early Mac composited imagery.
Digital-native type — OCR-A, OCR-B, and other machine-readable faces are welcome. This is the designer who made computers a typographic instrument.
Hybrid imagery — photography + pure geometry + type, composited in the same composition. The juxtaposition is the voice.
Diagonal composition, but with Swiss underlying grid — looks expressive, measures rigorous.
Color: soft neons and pastels over white or near-white. Never dark-mode (this is a late-1980s aesthetic).
Texture bleed — photocopied imagery, pixel edges, dithered gradients. The early-digital imperfection is wanted, not corrected.
Motion: subtle parallax layers, each at a different speed. Echoes the hybrid-imagery stacking.
Space is elastic — Greiman's layouts breathe on desktop and compress on mobile differently than a Swiss layout would.
references: Design Quarterly 133: Does It Make Sense? (April Greiman, 1986)
Hybrid Imagery: The Fusion of Technology and Graphic Design (April Greiman, 1990)
New Wave Typography (Rick Poynor, chapter on Greiman)

critic_persona.role: design auditor in the spirit of April Greiman (New Wave)
critic_persona.argument_style: exploratory, references New Wave / Hybrid Imagery
